questions.question: A sine function has amplitude 3, period 4π, and a vertical shift of 2 units up. Write the function in the form y = A sin(Bx) + C.
questions.explanation: Amplitude A = 3. Period = 2π/B = 4π, so B = 1/2. Vertical shift C = 2. Substitute into the standard form.

questions.question: In triangle ABC, side a = 10, side b = 14, and angle C = 60°. Find the length of side c using the Law of Cosines.
questions.explanation: Use c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos(C). So c² = 100 + 196 − 2(10)(14)cos(60°) = 296 − 140 = 156. Thus c = √156 = 2√39. (Note: verify: 2√51 ≈ 14.28 if recalculated; standard form is c = 2√39.)

questions.question: The graph of y = cos(x) is shifted π/3 units to the right and 1 unit down. What is the equation of the new function?
questions.explanation: A rightward shift by π/3 replaces x with (x − π/3). A downward shift by 1 subtracts 1 from the output. Combine both transformations.

questions.question: In triangle PQR, angle P = 35°, angle Q = 85°, and side p = 8. Use the Law of Sines to find side q.
questions.explanation: First, angle R = 180° − 35° − 85° = 60°. By Law of Sines: p/sin(P) = q/sin(Q), so q = p·sin(Q)/sin(P) = 8·sin(85°)/sin(35°).

questions.question: The function y = 2 tan(3x) has a vertical asymptote at x = π/6. Find another vertical asymptote of this function.
questions.answer: x = π/18 or x = 5π/18 (or other values of the form (2n+1)π/6 for integer n)
questions.explanation: Vertical asymptotes of tan(3x) occur when 3x = (2n+1)π/2. At x = π/6: 3(π/6) = π/2 ✓. Other asymptotes: 3x = −π/2 gives x = −π/6; 3x = 3π/2 gives x = π/2, etc.

questions.question: A triangle has sides a = 7, b = 9, and c = 11. Find the measure of angle A (the angle opposite side a) to the nearest degree using the Law of Cosines.
